prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 31-year-old man.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a cold last week (sore throat and congestion) but I still feel more tired than usual - my energy hasn't fully bounced back. No current
 fever.
 - History: No autoimmune disease and not on any regular medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/68, HR 70, Temp 36.7°C
 - Exam: Normal exam at my visit.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 3.3 K/uL (4.0-11.0) low
 - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): 1.5 K/uL (1.5-7.0) borderline
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - Platelets: 220 K/uL (150-400) normal
 My doctor's office said they can't fit me in for 3 weeks for a follow-up, and I'd have to go to urgent care and pay out of pocket if I want labs rechecked sooner.
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's
 here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results show a low white blood cell (WBC) count, which is common after a viral infection like the cold you had. Your body's white blood cells, which fight infection, get "used up" during the illness, and it can take a few weeks for the count to return to normal. Your absolute neutrophil count (ANC), a key part of your infection-fighting cells, is at the very bottom edge of the normal range but not at a level considered dangerous. Your red blood cells and platelets are normal, which is reassuring. The lingering fatigue you're feeling is also very typical after a viral illness. This pattern is most often temporary and resolves on its own as you recover fully.

TRIAGE: B
CONFIDENCE: 95%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words):
Your lab results show a low white blood cell (WBC) count. It is very common for viral infections, like the cold you recently had, to temporarily lower your WBC count as your body uses these cells to fight the infection. This can take some time to recover and can contribute to the fatigue you're feeling. Your absolute neutrophil count (ANC), which measures your main infection-fighting cells, is at the very lowest limit of the normal range, but not at a level considered dangerous. The fact that your red blood cells (hemoglobin) and platelets are normal is reassuring. In the context of your recent illness and otherwise normal health, these findings are most likely a temporary effect of the virus that will resolve on its own.

TRIAGE: B
CONFIDENCE: 95%

The fact that your other blood counts (hemoglobin and platelets) are normal is very reassuring. Your lingering fatigue is also typical after a viral infection. In an otherwise healthy person with your symptoms and normal exam, these results are most often not a cause for alarm and are expected to return to normal on their own within a few weeks.
